metadata.title: Why 'Claude is Private' Is Not the Privacy Question

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The nonprofit story reframed privacy for me: nothing malicious happened, yet the funder-report session's boundary covered client intake forms, salary sheets, and dispute emails because the grant covers what you included, not what you intended. Help me set my boundary before the session: (1) audit which of the three surfaces — Chat, Code, Cowork — reaches the most and why; (2) design a scoped folder that holds only the files a given task requires; (3) write the pre-session checklist of folder, files, apps, and forbidden actions so a grant never means everything inside it.

props.command: The nonprofit story reframed privacy for me: nothing malicious happened, yet the funder-report session's boundary covered client intake forms, salary sheets, and dispute emails because the grant covers what you included, not what you intended. Help me set my boundary before the session: (1) audit which of the three surfaces — Chat, Code, Cowork — reaches the most and why; (2) design a scoped folder that holds only the files a given task requires; (3) write the pre-session checklist of folder, files, apps, and forbidden actions so a grant never means everything inside it.

beats.narration_text: A nonprofit program manager opened Claude Cowork, pointed it at her Documents folder, and asked for help with a funder report. She did not think about what else lived in that folder.

production_viz.label: folder access grant — all files in scope
production_viz.mechanic: a folder chip (slate) labeled 'DOCUMENTS FOLDER' with a TEAL 'ACCESS GRANTED' badge; below it, three file chips: one TEAL labeled 'FUNDER REPORT', two CRIMSON labeled 'CLIENT INTAKE FILES' and 'STAFF SALARY DATA'

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] folder access grant — intended file and unintended files

beats.narration_text: The folder contained client intake forms with names and phone numbers, staff salary sheets, email exports from a dispute, and interview transcripts from a participant study. None of it intended for the session. All of it in scope.
beats.scene_description: office desk with a mixed pile of documents — grant materials visible on top, hidden below: a folder labeled with client names, salary sheets, personal files; access boundary implied by proximity; editorial newsprint desaturated treatment
beats.image_prompt: B02, office desk with a mixed stack of documents, grant report on top, underneath visible folders with sensitive labels, no legible text, editorial desaturated newsprint treatment, flat print lighting, collage aesthetic
beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] mixed document pile — grant materials with unintended sensitive files

beats.narration_text: Nothing malicious happened. No data was publicly exposed. But the access boundary covered everything in the folder — not only the funder report she meant to work on. Why didn't granting access mean granting access to the right files?

card.copy: Why didn't 'grant access' mean 'grant access to the right files'?

beats.narration_text: You might expect that a trusted AI platform manages the privacy boundary for you — that it knows which files are relevant, excludes sensitive ones automatically, and processes only what the task requires.

production_viz.label: naive model: platform manages the boundary automatically
production_viz.mechanic: folder chip (slate) with arrow to teal chip 'RELEVANT FILES ONLY'; label 'platform filters for you'; crimson chip 'SENSITIVE FILES' shown as excluded automatically

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] naive model — platform manages access boundary

beats.narration_text: Privacy for agentic AI is a configuration question — not a platform property. The access boundary is what you define before the session begins: which folder, which files, which apps, which actions. A grant covers everything inside the boundary. Not what you intended — what you included.

card.copy: Privacy is a configuration question, not a platform property.

beats.narration_text: The three Claude surfaces each define a different access boundary. Chat sees what you paste or upload. Code may see a code repository. Cowork may see local files, connected apps, a browser session, and scheduled tasks. Each surface expands what the AI can reach — and what the access boundary needs to cover.

production_viz.label: three surfaces — three access boundaries
production_viz.mechanic: three rows: CHAT / what you paste (small boundary, teal); CODE / code repository (medium boundary, teal); COWORK / files + apps + browser + tasks (large boundary, crimson if over-broad)

beats.narration_text: The access boundary is set by the user's choices before the session begins. Which folder. Which files. Which apps are allowed or forbidden. Nothing is excluded by default — only what you explicitly exclude.

card.copy: Nothing is excluded by default. Only what you explicitly exclude.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] default inclusion — the boundary is user-set

beats.narration_text: The program manager's session could have started differently. A dedicated working folder — grant materials only. Client files excluded. Salary data excluded. Transcripts excluded. The boundary set by what is in the folder, not by what the task is about.

production_viz.label: before vs after: broad grant vs scoped working folder
production_viz.mechanic: left BEFORE (crimson): Documents folder contains all files, boundary covers everything; right AFTER (teal): dedicated Claude-working folder contains only grant materials, others excluded

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] before vs after — broad grant vs scoped working folder

beats.narration_text: The practice move: before any Cowork session, create a dedicated working folder. Copy only the files the task needs. Remove anything personal, confidential, or sensitive. Name explicitly what Claude should not touch. Then point the session at that folder — not your main Documents folder.

production_viz.label: three steps before granting access
production_viz.mechanic: three teal chips in vertical list: 'CREATE DEDICATED WORKING FOLDER' / 'COPY ONLY WHAT THE TASK NEEDS' / 'NAME WHAT TO EXCLUDE'

beats.narration_text: The check: before granting access to any folder or app, ask — does everything inside this boundary belong in this session? If not, move it out or create a working copy with only what is needed. The boundary you set is the boundary Claude works within.

card.copy: Does everything in this boundary belong in this session?

beats.narration_text: Privacy is not a question about whether Claude is trustworthy. It is a question about what folder, what files, and what actions you configured before the session started.

card.copy: The question is not: is Claude private? The question is: what did I configure?

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] the configuration reframe — trustworthiness vs configuration

beats.narration_text: For the program manager: not the Documents folder — a working folder with grant materials only. The access grant is the privacy decision. Set it before the session begins.

production_viz.label: the working folder — only what belongs in the session
production_viz.mechanic: two folder chips side by side: DOCUMENTS FOLDER (crimson, large, labeled 'everything') and CLAUDE-WORKING (teal, small, labeled 'grant materials only'); arrow points from crimson to teal labeled 'set before the session'

beats.narration_text: The access grant is the privacy decision. Set the boundary before the session begins.
beats.scene_description: program manager at desk organizing a small dedicated folder with only a few documents, removing other files to a separate pile; deliberate, methodical; editorial newsprint desaturated treatment
beats.image_prompt: B13, program manager at desk organizing a small folder of documents, removing extra papers to a separate pile, deliberate methodical expression, editorial desaturated newsprint treatment, flat print lighting, no text legible, collage aesthetic
beats.new_visual_element: [PHOTO] program manager preparing a scoped working folder

beats.narration_text: Trusting the platform does not set the boundary. Trusting your own folder preparation does. The right question is not whether Claude is private. It is whether the folder, the files, and the actions you configured before the session are the ones that belong there.

card.copy: Trust your folder preparation, not just the platform.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] folder preparation is the trust layer

beats.narration_text: Privacy for agentic AI is a configuration question. Set the boundary before the session begins — what is in the folder is what Claude can see.

card.copy: Set the boundary before the session begins.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Trusting the platform does not set the boundary. Trusting your own folder preparation does. The right question is not whether Claude is private. It is whether the folder, the files, and the actions you configured before the session are the ones that belong there. Privacy for agentic AI is a configuration question. Set the boundary before the session begins — what is in the folder is what Claude can see.

props.artifactHeading: Why 'Claude is Private' Is Not the Privacy
artifactLines: The access grant is the privacy decision.
Trusting the platform does not set the boundary.
Privacy for agentic AI is a configuration question.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Why 'Claude is Private' Is Not the Privacy Question] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
